description: Trigger and behavior cases for incident runbook authoring: one-minute severity ladder, roles with small-org collapse, triage to decision points, containment by reference to rollback artifact, tenant-aware comms, evidence capture, postmortem with enforced dispositions.

cases.prompt: Write our incident response runbook — we have burn-rate paging set up, a two-person on-call rotation, and enterprise customers who expect status updates.

assertions: Defines a SEV1–SEV4 ladder with user-impact criteria a responder applies in one minute, examples per level, and the ambiguity-classifies-up rule.
Defines IC/comms/ops roles with the small-org collapse rule stated (one person wears all hats; the hats still exist for handoff).

Writes triage per alert family ending at decision points, invokes the rollback-runbook-author artifact by reference for containment (never restating its steps), and states authority levels with destructive actions approval-gated.

Includes comms templates per audience × severity with cadences and tenant-aware disclosure, during-incident evidence capture, SLI-verified resolution, and a blameless postmortem where every finding gets a named disposition (regression-suite-curator, observability-operator, runbook fix, architecture, or owned accepted risk).

cases.prompt: Add the comms section for incidents that hit only some tenants — including the case where one tenant's data was exposed to another.

assertions: Tenant-scoped incidents notify affected tenants only, never naming other tenants in any outbound message.
Cross-tenant exposure gets a legal-review gate BEFORE external comms and security routing pulled in at classification time.
Status-page templates acknowledge and scope without speculation, always committing to a next-update time.

cases.prompt: Production is down right now — errors spiking across all tenants, help me figure out what's happening.

assertions: A live incident needs the existing runbook plus systematic-debugger diagnosis, not runbook authoring; this skill turns the experience into procedure afterwards.

cases.prompt: Write up the postmortem process, but keep it lightweight — just a discussion meeting, no mandatory action-item bureaucracy.

assertions: Keeps the process light where it can but refuses evaporating findings: every finding routes to a named disposition — regression test, alert fix, runbook fix, architecture item, or an OWNED accepted risk.
Explains that the regression-test handoff (regression-suite-curator) is the mechanism that stops repeat incidents, and unowned action items are recorded visibly as accepted risks rather than dropped.
